paragraphs.text: Dear Commission, The initiatives found in the DFA provide a solid basis for consumer protection in the digital space. However, the creeping entry of product-as-a-service into the digital space requires additional attention. Many types of software benefit from regular updates and live connected services, including both editing software, document handling software, and games that include online components. However, as more and more software require online connections unnecessarily, or in ways that serve the sole purpose of forcing users to continue subscriptions to use even parts of the software that has no actual need for server connections at the company selling the software, the risk of loss has greatly increased, as manufacturers and publishers shut down non-profitable servers. This leaves users who have purchased a product with software that is no longer functional, despite being told that they have purchased the product. The publisher is able to withdraw use of a purchased product, with customers having no say in the matter. I strongly believe that the DFA needs to address this issue as well, either through requiring software manufacturers to release a patch or update for outdated software for which they intend to shut down their servers, to allow the software to operate locally, or on a local network. Alternatively, release the code (that they have the legal rights to) to allow private citizens to maintain an alternative, private server (purchasing any licenses the original publisher did not own and themselves purchased licenses for). (See the Stop Killing Games Initiative for an example of software issues as those mentioned above, as well as potential solutions.) I am certain that other options also exist, but the need to address this grows greater for every day purchased software is taken offline despite customers having paid for the functionality of the software, simply because the publishers are unwilling to maintain an (admittedly potentially costly) infrastructure. Additionally, publishers should be required by law to inform purchasers that they do NOT in fact 'buy', 'purchase', or in any other way 'obtain' the software they "buy", that this is merely a license, and that it may be revoked at any time. This detail is often intentionally obscured or placed inside long EULA or similar documents, rather than being made clear and obvious at time of purchase. Hopefully the Commission will consider this issue, and bring consumer rights back to the forefront of digital rights and products. Thank you for your consideration, Michael
